meta.title: Atlas for Mac — Explainable, recovery-first Mac maintenance
meta.description: Atlas is an open-source macOS maintenance workspace. Every scan is itemized, every action enters the ledger, and remains restorable within the retention window.

hero.headline: Mac maintenance — calm, explainable, recoverable
hero.subheadline: Atlas is an open-source macOS maintenance workspace. Every action produces a plan, is itemized for review, and enters the ledger — restorable item by item within the retention window. Nothing is silently deleted.
hero.prereleaseWarning: Prerelease builds are for testing only and may contain unknown issues. Do not use them in production.
hero.gatekeeperNote: First launch requires a right-click → Open to pass macOS Gatekeeper verification.

scenarios.before: Cleaners delete files without telling you what was removed
scenarios.after: Atlas lists every item in a plan, runs only after you confirm, and records it to the ledger

scenarios.before: A config file gets deleted and there's nothing to find or restore
scenarios.after: Atlas creates a restore point for each action and lets you recover item by item from the ledger (when supported)

scenarios.before: Tools demand Full Disk Access without explaining why
scenarios.after: Atlas requests permissions on demand, explains each one, and runs basics without Full Disk Access

cards.title: Overview
cards.value: Your Mac's health on one screen
cards.example: Disk usage, cache ratio, reclaimable space — at a glance

cards.title: Smart Clean
cards.value: Scan, review the plan, run on confirm
cards.example: System caches 3.2 GB — review the breakdown before cleaning
cards.trustCue: Restore point created before run; rollback supported

cards.title: File Organizer
cards.value: File scattered files into place by rules
cards.example: Auto-sort Downloads by type/date/project, preview then run
cards.trustCue: Moves, never deletes — and still enters the ledger

cards.title: Apps
cards.value: Map each app's full footprint and plan uninstall
cards.example: See every related file an app leaves on disk
cards.trustCue: Lists all targets before uninstall, item by item

cards.title: Ledger
cards.value: A traceable timeline of every action
cards.example: Plan №N · scan receipt · restore point — all numbered on the record
cards.trustCue: Every action is traceable; nothing happens silently

cards.title: Permissions
cards.example: Requests access only when scanning a protected location
cards.trustCue: Every permission comes with a stated purpose

steps.label: Scan
steps.description: Atlas reads your disk and identifies caches, logs, and leftovers to produce a plan.

steps.label: Review
steps.description: The plan is itemized with explanations and sizes — you decide what stays or goes.

steps.label: Run
steps.description: On confirm, Atlas creates a restore point and records the action to the ledger as plan №N.

steps.label: Restore
steps.description: Within the retention window, recover cleaned items one by one from the ledger (when supported).

ledger.subtitle: Atlas makes trust structural: scans produce plans, runs enter the ledger, restore points are real. The number, receipt, evidence, and recovery path aren't marketing — they're things you can click.
ledger.footnote: Item-level restore within the retention window; some cases (e.g. snapshot-covered items) may not be supported.

developer.subtitle: Atlas recognizes dev toolchains and avoids deleting things that matter.

items.title: Toolchain-aware
items.description: Detects caches and artifacts from Homebrew, Xcode, Node.js and more, flagging each as safe or cautionary.

items.title: Project directory protection
items.description: Sees .git, node_modules, build and similar directories and excludes them by default to keep your workflow intact.

items.title: CLI integration
items.description: Ships a CLI for scripted scan and clean, ready to drop into automation.

items.title: Transparent ledger logs
items.description: Every action is recorded under its plan number with paths, sizes, and timestamps for easy auditing.

safety.subtitle: Atlas's design principles: explain before acting, recover after.

points.title: Preview before acting
points.description: Every clean shows the full target list first — there is no black-box one-click clean.

points.title: File-level backup
points.description: Cleaned files are backed up for the retention window and recoverable item by item from the ledger (when supported).

points.title: Least privilege
points.description: Atlas requests specific permissions only when needed, each with a stated purpose. Basics run without Full Disk Access.

gatekeeperGuide.title: Opening Atlas for the first time
steps: Download the Atlas .dmg from this site
Drag Atlas into your Applications folder
Right-click the Atlas icon and choose Open
Confirm with Open in the dialog

items.caption: Smart Clean — scan, review the plan, run on confirm

items.question: Is Atlas signed and notarized?
items.answer: Atlas is signed with an Apple Developer ID and passes Apple's notarization. On first launch, macOS Gatekeeper verifies integrity automatically. Prerelease builds may not be notarized and require a manual right-click → Open.

items.question: What happens after installing a prerelease?
items.answer: Prerelease builds may contain unfinished features or known issues and are for testing only. Because they aren't Apple-notarized, the first launch needs a right-click → Open. Don't rely on them on important machines.

items.question: Does Atlas upload my files?
items.answer: No. Atlas runs entirely on-device and never collects or uploads files or personal data. Scan results and ledger entries stay local. The source code is fully open for review.

items.question: What do 'ledger' and 'recoverable' actually mean?
items.answer: Before cleaning, Atlas backs up the targets to a local retention directory and records the action on the ledger under a plan number (№N) with paths, sizes, and a restore point. Within the retention window (default 30 days) you can recover cleaned items one by one from the ledger. This is file-level backup, not a system snapshot, so some cases may not be supported.

items.question: Does Atlas need Full Disk Access?
items.answer: Basics run without Full Disk Access. Atlas follows least privilege — it only requests a permission when scanning a specific protected location, and explains the purpose first.

items.question: Is Atlas on the Mac App Store?
items.answer: Not today. Atlas is distributed via direct download to keep full system-maintenance capability. The Mac App Store sandbox would limit core clean and restore features. A slimmed-down version may be considered later.
